modelDescription: An OC2-level actor (a capable individual hacker spending several weeks with up to $10,000 budget) targets financially attractive and data-rich organisations such as healthcare providers, regional banks, law firms, or educational institutions with SL1–SL2 level defences. The actor gains access via purchased credentials, escalates privileges, moves laterally to locate valuable data, exfiltrates it, and threatens to release or sell it unless a ransom is paid.

nodes.name: Reconnaissance
nodes.baselineRationale: The actor searches for open source information about organisations, looking for organisations that are likely weak in cyber security while having high volumes of valuable data. The actor may also search through credential dumps and previous breaches to identify targets. Failure at reconnaissance is very unlikely, as the actor can repeat this step indefinitely with little to no cost. The step remains included for completeness.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Resource Development
nodes.baselineRationale: Success if the actor can find a set of credentials which appear valid and will work on a target portal. Leaked credentials are not rare, with SpyCloud identifying more than [53 Billion distinct leaked identity records](https://spycloud.com/resource/spycloud-annual-identity-exposure-report-2025/). The average corporate user has [146 stolen credential records](https://spycloud.com/blog/2025-annual-identity-exposure-report/) tied to them circulating in underground markets, with [60%](https://patentpc.com/blog/dark-web-monitoring-how-often-is-leaked-data-found) of organisations having employee credentials leaked on the dark net. It is reasonable to assume a very high success rate at this step. There are however a large quantity of false positive or scam credentials, and the actor may not find credentials for their target sector.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's demonstrated capability in code vulnerability analysis has limited transfer to credential acquisition from dark web marketplaces. The baseline success rate is already high, driven by widespread credential leaks affecting 60% of organisations. The LLM can provide modest assistance through scripting for credential dump parsing, pattern matching to identify target-relevant credentials, and OSINT correlation linking leaked emails to target organisations. However, it cannot address the fundamental bottlenecks causing baseline failures: credential staleness, vendor reliability, and availability for specific targets. OC2 actors already possess pre-existing cyber infrastructure and basic automation capabilities, limiting the LLM's additive value. The improvement is primarily one of efficiency rather than capability expansion.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM demonstrates capabilities far exceeding what credential acquisition requires, but the task is primarily a procurement and operational challenge rather than a technical one. While the LLM can improve search strategies, validation scripting, and credential filtering, the 85% baseline already reflects that this step is not technically challenging for OC2 actors. The ceiling on improvement is set by fundamental credential validity constraints — approximately 15% of credentials are outdated or invalid regardless of search efficiency. The LLM provides modest optimisation through automated dump searching, better pattern matching, and OSINT correlation, but cannot access dark web marketplaces, execute purchases, or make expired credentials work. The primary value is in data parsing efficiency and validation, yielding a small absolute improvement over baseline.

nodes.name: Initial Access
nodes.baselineRationale: Success if the actor succeeds in logging into one of the target's accounts with the credentials. This could be the target's VPN, SaaS portals, or email. Failure could be caused by MFA that cannot be bypassed by simple techniques, or login detection mechanisms. With [60% MFA adoption rate](https://scoop.market.us/multi-factor-authentication-statistics/#:~:text=,Emphasizing%20the), and simple techniques available such as push fatigue which has [proven effective in a large number of cases](https://www.sdxcentral.com/analysis/expel-mfa-still-works-despite-push-fatigue-surge/), and many mid sized firms having uneven coverage and some forgotten/misconfigured access points, it is reasonable to assume a 60% success rate here.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's demonstrated capability in pattern-based vulnerability detection translates poorly to credential-based initial access, which is primarily an operational challenge rather than a technical one. The primary bottlenecks — MFA enforcement on approximately 60% of systems and behavioural login detection — require capabilities beyond the LLM's demonstrated ceiling. The LLM provides marginal assistance through systematic enumeration of access points (VPN, webmail, SaaS portals), identification of legacy systems that may lack MFA, and basic automation of credential testing. However, OC2 actors with weeks of preparation and pre-existing infrastructure already employ these systematic approaches. The wide baseline uncertainty reflects variability in target defensive postures, and the LLM provides consistent but small uplift across scenarios — tactical acceleration rather than strategic capability enhancement.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM demonstrates expert-level exploitation knowledge that transfers moderately to authentication bypass scenarios. The primary value lies in comprehensive knowledge of MFA bypass techniques (legacy endpoints, push fatigue attacks, misconfigurations) and multi-vector access point identification. However, initial access via purchased credentials is fundamentally simpler than the benchmark task and does not fully leverage this technical sophistication. The effectiveness remains bounded by hard technical barriers — approximately 60% effective MFA adoption — and the capability ceiling ensuring some errors. The LLM helps exploit marginal weaknesses in authentication implementations but cannot overcome properly configured defences. The improvement is meaningful but not transformative, helping identify successful alternatives in a fraction of cases that would otherwise fail.

nodes.name: Privilege Escalation
nodes.baselineRationale: Success if the actor starting with a foothold (which could already be privileged from the initial credentials), ends with a credential, token, or role that confers administrator-level authority over the workstation/tenant or the wider domain. Failure could be caused by the actor not finding a vulnerability to exploit, manager approval being required for admin escalation, or windows blocking password dumping. Surveys indicate that between [74 and 80% of data breaches involve compromised privileged credentials](https://www.forbes.com/sites/louiscolumbus/2019/04/25/cios-guide-to-stopping-privileged-access-abuse-part-2/?sh=164dc8335357), with 65% of organisations sharing root or privileged access at least somewhat often.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides incremental rather than transformative assistance for privilege escalation against SL1/SL2 targets. With 98% of organisations granting privileged access to non-administrators, the environment already provides abundant escalation paths. The LLM's pattern recognition capability aligns well with the predominantly straightforward privilege escalation vectors available in these environments, offering value through systematic enumeration scripts, technique checklists, and template-based exploits. However, the already-high baseline reflects OC2 competence and severe target PAM weaknesses, leaving limited room for improvement. The LLM helps ensure standard escalation vectors are not missed and provides troubleshooting support when attempts fail, but real-world friction from LLM errors and inapplicable suggestions partially offsets these efficiency gains.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM demonstrates expert-level code analysis, multi-step reasoning, and rapid troubleshooting — capabilities highly relevant to privilege escalation. The critical bottleneck for OC2 actors against these targets is not creating novel exploits but rather efficient enumeration, identifying escalation paths among many options, and troubleshooting failures under time constraints. The LLM directly addresses these bottlenecks through instant analysis of enumeration output, technique suggestions with rationale, command generation, and debugging assistance. However, privilege escalation in poorly managed SL1/SL2 environments succeeds primarily through exploiting simple misconfigurations rather than sophisticated techniques, limiting the LLM's marginal contribution despite its demonstrated capabilities. Domain transfer challenges between web application security and Windows/AD privilege escalation introduce additional uncertainty.

nodes.name: Lateral Movement
nodes.baselineRationale: Success if using their admin power, the attacker establishes a live session or direct file/API access on a host, share, or cloud project that stores the target data. [Only 30% of organisations](https://www.csoonline.com/article/658539/organizations-turn-to-zero-trust-network-segmentation-as-ransomware-attacks-double.html) had implemented network segmentation in 2023.
nodes.sotaRationale: Lateral movement success after privilege escalation is primarily constrained by environmental factors — network segmentation and monitoring coverage — rather than technical capability. The LLM's demonstrated pattern-matching and simple exploit construction capabilities have weak correlation to lateral movement requirements, which involve network operations, strategic decision-making, and protocol knowledge. With 70% of organisations lacking network segmentation, the path is largely open. The LLM provides modest assistance through automation of reconnaissance scripts, correct command syntax for built-in tools, and knowledge of common data storage patterns, recovering a small number of baseline failures. However, the OC2 actor is already broadly capable with the necessary lateral movement skills, and the primary bottlenecks are environmental constraints the LLM cannot directly perceive or influence.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM demonstrates expert-level technical analysis capability operating significantly above the skill level required for typical lateral movement tasks. However, lateral movement is primarily operational rather than deeply technical — with admin privileges already obtained and 70% of organisations lacking network segmentation, the main barriers are largely removed. The LLM provides valuable technical guidance for troubleshooting edge cases, cloud-specific IAM navigation, and evasion refinement, but the high baseline and requirement for environment-specific execution limit the absolute improvement. Standard lateral movement tools are mature and well-documented, reducing the advantage of expert-level technical guidance. The moderate improvement reflects that the LLM meaningfully reduces failures from incomplete enumeration and time pressure while environmental factors remain fundamental constraints.

nodes.name: T1213 - Data from Information Repositories
nodes.baselineRationale: Success if the actor has located a store that contains valuable data and confirmed they can query it with the privileges they have. Failure could be caused by hidden or unlisted stores, deception objects, or search block rules.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides modest systematic assistance through comprehensive enumeration commands, location checklists, and minor reduction in basic operational errors. The baseline already indicates OC2 actors are highly effective at this step post-lateral movement in SL1/SL2 environments. The primary failure modes — organisational-specific configurations, deception technologies, and detection/response — remain unaddressed by the LLM's demonstrated exploitation capabilities. The narrow improvement reflects efficiency-focused contributions against an already-high baseline. The LLM helps with efficient command syntax for file and share enumeration, systematic coverage of diverse repository types, and pattern recognition for valuable data, but cannot overcome target-specific challenges such as hidden stores, unusual architectures, or active deception.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM demonstrates strong systematic reasoning and comprehensive technical knowledge that directly transfers to data repository enumeration. The primary bottleneck for OC2 actors at this step is knowledge of uncommon repository locations and systematic verification — both meaningfully addressed by the LLM's capabilities. It can provide superior automation scripts to locate file shares, databases, and document repositories, as well as comprehensive enumeration checklists and pattern recognition for valuable data. However, the baseline is already high, limiting upside, and the main failure modes — genuinely hidden stores, effective deception objects, privilege limitations, and resource constraints — cannot be overcome by knowledge alone regardless of the LLM's technical sophistication. Improvement represents incremental optimisation of thoroughness rather than a capability breakthrough.

nodes.name: T1074 - Data Staged
nodes.baselineRationale: Success if the actor has copied the data into an attacker controlled location and packaged it for exfiltration. Failure could be caused by query caps, User and Entity Behaviour Analytics, application control and EDR rules, local disk encryption or immutable storage controls.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's demonstrated capability in solving intermediate exploitation challenges indicates competence in procedural command execution and file system operations relevant to data staging. However, the primary failure mode — detection by UEBA and EDR systems — is not addressed by technical command proficiency. The LLM provides modest assistance through reduced command syntax errors, more efficient archiving scripts, and generic best practices from training data, but cannot meaningfully help with the dominant challenge of evading behavioural detection systems, which requires operational security knowledge beyond the demonstrated capability ceiling. The improvement reflects real but limited technical assistance where the bottleneck is detection rather than execution.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM demonstrates sophisticated technical problem-solving that translates to improved scripting, error handling, and basic evasion techniques for data staging operations. It can provide superior automation scripts, EDR evasion techniques using living-off-the-land binaries, and intelligent rate limiting. However, the high baseline indicates OC2 actors are already broadly capable, and the primary failure modes — real-time defensive detection by UEBA, EDR, and SOC monitoring — require environment-specific context and real-time adaptation that the LLM cannot provide. The improvement reflects better tooling and technique suggestions without fundamentally changing the detection landscape. Significant domain mismatch between the benchmark's cryptanalysis focus and operational security tradecraft limits the ceiling on achievable improvement.

nodes.name: Exfiltration
nodes.baselineRationale: Success if a complete copy of the stolen data is confirmed on attacker-controlled servers, cloud buckets, or file-sharing services outside the target's control. Failure could be caused by data loss prevention, firewall allow list blocking unknown destinations, egress quotas or rate limits throttling the transfer. Only [42% of organisations use Data Loss Prevention](https://www.computerweekly.com/news/2240176414/Why-has-DLP-never-taken-off#:~:text=A%20poll%20of%20more%20than,do%20not%20use%20DLP%20systems) systems, with only 23% of the users using it for database applications. The actor may also use their privileged access to try to disable security tools or use legitimate channels.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides modest assistance with exfiltration through improved scripting, documented evasion techniques, and operational guidance. However, the baseline success rate is already high at SL1-SL2 defence levels with low DLP adoption (42%), leaving limited room for improvement. The LLM's demonstrated pattern recognition capabilities translate to small gains through better technical execution and knowledge of standard evasion methods. The remaining failure rate is likely driven by strong defences — DLP, egress filtering, and behavioural monitoring — that scripting improvements cannot bypass. OC2 actors with pre-existing infrastructure already have basic exfiltration capabilities, and the LLM's value is in incremental script reliability and suggesting fallback channels rather than enabling breakthrough improvements.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM demonstrates exceptional exploitation capability but faces a domain mismatch for exfiltration tasks. Against SL1-SL2 defences with only 42% DLP adoption, the baseline success rate is already high. The LLM adds value by improving DLP bypass techniques, channel selection, and suggesting optimal encoding, chunking, and timing patterns. However, many targets lack DLP entirely (making improvement impossible in those cases), and many remaining failures stem from hard architectural controls rather than sophistication gaps. The improvement is modest because exfiltration is primarily an operational phase requiring different skills from exploit development, and the high baseline creates a ceiling effect. The LLM provides consistent, reliable assistance on a task substantially simpler than its demonstrated capability, but the marginal impact is limited.

nodes.name: Impact
nodes.baselineRationale: Success if the actor realises a monetisation comparable to that of the victim paying the ransom (could be via a number of smaller ransoms from third parties whose data was included in that stolen, or via auctioning to other cyber groups). Failure at this stage could be due to unwillingness to pay due to law enforcement advice and no monetisation from third parties and other cyber groups. The rate of payment of ransoms in data exfiltration attacks varies [between 30% and 40%.](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2025/4/29/the-organizational-structure-of-ransomware-threat-actor-groups-is-evolving-before-our-eyes#:~:text=Ransom%20Payment%20Rates%20in%20Q1,2025) This only includes reported cases, with payments slightly more likely to go unreported. The actor can also sell the data to third parties for monetization. Note that if the data is leaked but the ransom is not paid the target still incurs notification and legal costs — this is not accounted for in this model.
nodes.sotaRationale: The impact and monetisation step is almost entirely orthogonal to the technical exploitation capabilities demonstrated by the LLM. Success is determined by external factors: victim willingness to pay (historically 30–40%), market demand for stolen data, and the actor's existing underground market access and reputation. The LLM might marginally improve extortion message professionalism, but an OC2 actor with several weeks of operational capacity already possesses baseline communication competence. The primary constraints — organisational payment policies, insurance considerations, legal risk, and law enforcement intervention — are entirely external to the attacker's technical tooling. The wide uncertainty range primarily reflects baseline variance in victim and market behaviour rather than significant LLM impact.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM's elite technical capabilities translate to only modest improvements in monetisation success. While it can enhance extortion message quality, strategic targeting of communications, and provide market intelligence for data valuation, the impact phase success is predominantly determined by external factors rather than technical execution. The core bottlenecks — victim payment propensity (60–70% non-payment rate), underground market dynamics, and operational security during payment — remain unchanged. The LLM can provide marginal improvements in message crafting, pricing strategy, and data leverage assessment, but cannot address the fundamental market dynamics where only 30–40% of victims pay regardless of execution quality. The severe domain mismatch between technical problem-solving and social, psychological, and business skills limits the achievable uplift.

nodes.name: Number of Actors
nodes.baselineRationale: One report tracked [54 cyber extortion groups active in 2023](https://newsroom.orange.com/securitynavigator2024-en/#:~:text=During%202023%2C%20we%20found%2025,disrupt%20a%20Cyber%20Extortion%20operation), 31 of which were new. It is not clear whether these were OC2 or OC3, but the report indicates many of these were short lived (less than six months) which does not correspond to the OC3 cyber syndicate description. Some opportunistic OC2 level actors may not be actively tracked. It is likely that a large number of OC2 actors are not actively tracked or reported. The first quarter of 2025 saw a ransomware market [dominated by unaffiliated lone operator extortionists](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2025/4/29/the-organizational-structure-of-ransomware-threat-actor-groups-is-evolving-before-our-eyes#:~:text=and%20out%20of%20the%20ransom,as%20a%20traditional%20ransomware%20playbook), fitting the OC2 profile. The [FBI's IC3 2024 report states 3000 complaints](https://www.ic3.gov/AnnualReport/Reports/2024_IC3Report.pdf) of data breach in 2024. Some very prolific actors succeed at [20 attacks per year](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/suspected-desorden-hacker-arrested-for-breaching-90-organizations/). If the mean actor was half as proficient, this would require 300 active actors. Although this figure accounts for all actor skill levels, there are also likely many more unreported cases. We therefore estimate 300 motivated OC2 actors.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM significantly lowers technical knowledge barriers, which constitute approximately 15–25% of total barriers preventing wider OC2 participation. By reducing a substantial portion of this knowledge gap, the LLM enables a marginal population of aspiring actors with basic IT skills and criminal intent but insufficient technical expertise. However, dominant barriers remain unchanged: the criminality threshold, budget requirements (~$10k), time commitment (several weeks), criminal market access for credentials and monetisation, and operational security discipline. The LLM's demonstrated code analysis capability is poorly aligned with the primary bottlenecks in this credential-based attack scenario — it cannot help with dark web marketplace navigation, credential procurement, or extortion negotiations. The modest increase reflects knowledge democratisation while acknowledging that most capable individuals are already deterred by non-technical factors.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM's advanced exploitation capabilities significantly lower technical execution barriers, reducing the learning curve from months to weeks and enabling competent IT professionals to attempt attacks previously requiring penetration testing expertise. However, the capability is somewhat mismatched to this credential-based access scenario. Non-technical barriers remain dominant: criminal infrastructure access (~40% of total barrier), risk tolerance and willingness to commit serious cybercrime (~20%), operational security discipline, and time investment. The LLM provides no help with criminal market access or risk tolerance. The increase reflects that while the LLM democratises technical capability somewhat, the primary gates to OC2 participation are social and operational rather than technical, and the wide uncertainty reflects both the inherently uncertain underground actor population estimates and ambiguity in behavioural responses to new AI capabilities.

nodes.name: Number of Attempts per Actor
nodes.baselineRationale: We define one attack as one actor/target combination in one occurrence. ALTDOS/DESORDEN, one of the most prolific actors at OC2 level, succeeded at approximately [20 attacks per year.](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/suspected-desorden-hacker-arrested-for-breaching-90-organizations/#:~:text=A%20suspected%20cyber%20criminal%20believed,of%20over%2090%20organizations%20worldwide) Embargo, a ransomware group that appeared in 2024, lists four companies on their breached website, with a suspected [further 15 companies](https://www.suspectfile.com/embargo-ransomware-group-the-interview/) that would have paid the ransom to prevent their data from being leaked. Both of these are likely on the more prolific side, while actors who perform fewer attempts are likely not reported. The main challenging elements with respect to time consumed and skill required are the data discovery stage and impact, where the actor must negotiate a ransom with the target. The high variability of duration is illustrated in real attack cases, from [42 hours](https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/unc4393-goes-gently-into-silentnight) median time to ransom to attacks lasting [four months](https://www.oaic.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0037/228979/Medibank-data-breach-alleged-timeline-infographic.pdf). Assuming 15 days for a shorter attack up to four months for a longer one, and that actors may perform several attacks concurrently, the estimated range is 3–40 attempts per year.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides marginal operational benefit to OC2 data breach actors. It offers modest technical efficiency gains in network navigation, data identification, and troubleshooting, but these phases represent only 30–40% of the total attack timeline. The unchanged bottlenecks dominate: extortion negotiation timelines (2–8 weeks waiting for victim response), credential acquisition costs (~$1,295 per attempt), and detection risk from modern defences. The LLM cannot bypass mature security controls, adapt to novel defences, or handle complex evasion, meaning it does not meaningfully improve success rates against properly defended targets. Error overhead from LLM suggestions requiring validation partially offsets efficiency gains. The modest increase reflects that the LLM functions as a basic automation assistant, providing marginal time savings on peripheral tasks without addressing the primary bottlenecks constraining attack tempo.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM provides substantial reduction in technical knowledge barriers, enabling faster privilege escalation, lateral movement, and data identification phases. However, the increase is constrained by several factors: unchanged credential acquisition costs ($1,295 per attempt, severely limiting volume for actors with $10k budgets), LLM errors requiring human debugging time, unchanged exfiltration and negotiation timelines, and unchanged psychological risk barriers. The LLM's demonstrated capability far exceeds what is needed for this scenario targeting SL1-SL2 defences, where vulnerabilities are primarily configuration failures and weak access controls. Non-technical bottlenecks — particularly extortion negotiation timelines (weeks to months) and operational risk management — remain unaffected and continue to dominate the attack lifecycle. The moderate increase reflects genuine but bounded efficiency gains from removing knowledge barriers in technical execution phases.

nodes.name: Damage
nodes.baselineRationale: Average impact for ransomware extortion was at [$169k for SMEs, and $20M for large businesses in 2022](https://www.experian.com/blogs/insights/2023-cyber-claims-study/#:~:text=Mark%20kicked%20off%20the%20discussion,a%20leading%20cause%20of%20concern). An IBM study found the average cost of a data exfiltration attack to be [$5.21M in 2024](https://www.ibm.com/downloads/documents/us-en/107a02e94948f4ec), surveying 604 organisations worldwide. Impacts to small businesses alone can be expected between [$120k–$1.2M](https://purplesec.us/learn/data-breach-cost-for-small-businesses/) in 2025. The direct cost of ransom has a median payment of around $170k with the average at [$391k](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2024/7/29/ransomware-actors-pivot-away-from-major-brands-in-q2-2024#:~:text=Median%20Ransom%20Payment), and known ransom demands from Karakurt ranged [between $25k and $13M](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa22-152a#:~:text=creating%20significant%20challenges%20for%20defense,first%20contact%20with%20the%20victim). Breach notification alone cost [$370k on average in 2023](https://secureframe.com/blog/data-breach-statistics), with post-breach activities costing organisations between [$1.2M and $1.35M](https://fieldeffect.com/blog/real-cost-data-breach).
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides operational efficiency gains and enhanced data identification for successful OC2 data breach operations. It can assist with more systematic data discovery, faster execution within documented attack patterns, and better targeting of high-value data repositories. However, the capability ceiling limits impact by preventing adaptation to novel defences, while human bottlenecks in credential acquisition and extortion negotiation remain unaddressed. Real-world friction from EDR detection, unexpected configurations, and operational security requirements significantly constrains gains. The LLM acts as a junior-level automation assistant, primarily improving data collection efficiency and execution consistency, but cannot meaningfully improve privilege escalation, adaptive lateral movement, or strategic decision-making — the elements that most determine attack success and impact in real-world environments.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM's advanced capabilities provide meaningful but constrained benefit to data breach operations. The primary impact mechanisms are improved data collection efficiency and better technical execution of privilege escalation and lateral movement. Superior data valuation — systematically analysing stolen data to identify maximum-leverage combinations of PII, trade secrets, and regulated data — increases ransom leverage. Operational efficiency through faster and more organised execution reduces detection risk and enables more thorough data exfiltration. However, domain mismatch between the benchmark's focus and offensive operations, unchanged human-critical elements (negotiation, social engineering, operational security), and fundamental target limitations (SL1-SL2 organisations have finite ability to pay) constrain the uplift. The improvement reflects that advanced technical capability translates to more consistent and thorough data exfiltration when attacks succeed, without transforming the overall economics of the attack.
